content.text: Long-running agents are AI workflows that execute for minutes, hours, or even days — far beyond the lifetime of a single HTTP request. They cycle through plan-act-observe loops, call external tools, accumulate partial results, and must survive crashes, network blips, and worker restarts without losing work.
is an AI process whose execution time is measured in minutes to hours rather than milliseconds. It is decoupled from any synchronous client connection: the client submits a job, receives a

immediately, and later polls for results or receives a webhook when the job finishes. The agent itself runs in a background worker — looping through tool calls, LLM invocations, and state updates — until the goal is reached or a timeout fires.

Examples include research pipelines that search dozens of sources and synthesise a report, coding agents that write and iteratively debug a codebase, data-processing agents that clean and annotate thousands of records, and multi-step approval workflows that pause for human input in the middle of execution.

Most valuable AI tasks cannot fit inside a single HTTP timeout. A web server typically enforces a 30–60 second request limit. An LLM call that generates a long document already risks that limit; an agent that makes a dozen tool calls, each potentially waiting for a slow external API, absolutely exceeds it. Without a long-running execution model you are forced to either artificially split the task (losing coherent state between pieces) or block the user for a frustratingly long time.
. The service remains responsive — accepting new jobs while others run — and the infrastructure can scale workers independently of the front-end.
execution time is limited only by business logic, not transport layer defaults.
persisting state after each step means a restarted worker picks up from the last checkpoint, not from the beginning.
the client receives a job ID in milliseconds and continues other work while the agent runs.
add more workers to increase throughput without touching the API layer.
partial results and heartbeats give real-time visibility into what the agent is doing, unlike an opaque synchronous call.
the agent can pause, emit a question, wait for approval, and resume — all within the same logical execution.
you need a queue, a worker pool, and a state store — more moving parts than a simple API handler.
agents that loop many times accumulate LLM token costs; an open-ended loop without a step budget can surprise you.
distributed state means you need structured logs and trace IDs to follow execution across restarts.
each step must be safe to retry — side effects like sending emails or writing to a DB must be deduplicated.
the client cannot consume a streaming response; it either polls or receives a single webhook at the end.
Think of a long-running agent as a

. When a customer orders, the waiter (API) hands a ticket to the kitchen and returns immediately. The chef reads the ticket, executes each step of the recipe, and when done, rings a bell (webhook) or the waiter periodically checks the pass-through window (polling). If the chef takes a break, the ticket and all completed prep work remain — the next chef to come on shift picks up exactly where they left off.
state lives in the store, not in the worker process
. Any worker can execute any step as long as it can read the persisted state.
Client POSTs a job request; the API handler creates a task record in a state store (e.g., Redis, Postgres) with status
A message is pushed to a job queue (Redis, SQS, RabbitMQ) with the
A worker dequeues the message, updates status to
, and begins the agent loop: Plan → Act → Observe → repeat.
After each step the worker writes a
(timestamp + partial results) to the state store. If the worker crashes, the queue's visibility timeout eventually re-delivers the message to another worker.
When the agent finishes, the worker writes the final result with status
or receives the webhook to retrieve the result.

The diagram below shows the full request-to-result flow: a client enqueues a job, a worker executes a Plan-Act-Observe loop while heartbeating state, calls external tools, and persists results for the client to retrieve.

attrs.alt: Long-running agent architecture: Client enqueues job, Worker runs Plan-Act-Observe loop with heartbeats to State Store, calls External Tools, client polls for result

content.text: Tasks that take longer than 30 seconds end-to-end — research, code generation, document processing.
Workflows that call multiple external APIs, each of which may be slow or rate-limited.
Pipelines where partial results are valuable before completion (e.g., stream intermediate findings to a dashboard).
Processes that require human approval at an intermediate step before continuing.
Batch operations on large datasets that you want to run without blocking.
. The API handler writes to both the state store and the queue; it never runs the agent itself.

import httpx, time

base = "http://localhost:8000"

# Submit
resp = httpx.post(f"{base}/tasks", json={"goal": "Research the history of transformer models and write a 500-word summary."})
task_id = resp.json()["task_id"]
print(f"Task submitted: {task_id}")

# Poll until done
while True:
    status_resp = httpx.get(f"{base}/tasks/{task_id}")
    data = status_resp.json()
    print(f"Status: {data['status']}")
    if data["status"] in ("DONE", "FAILED"):
        print(data["result"])
        break
    time.sleep(3)

A legal document review service uses long-running agents to analyse contracts. A lawyer uploads a PDF and submits a task. The agent extracts clauses, cross-references each clause against a regulatory database (three separate HTTP calls), runs a risk-scoring LLM call per clause, and aggregates a report. The entire pipeline takes 8–12 minutes. The lawyer receives an email (webhook-triggered) when the report is ready. Without the async task pattern, the HTTP connection would have timed out after 60 seconds and left both the lawyer and the system in an undefined state.
Running the agent inside the API handler:
this blocks the request thread and ties execution lifetime to the HTTP connection. Any timeout or client disconnect kills the agent.
guard can loop indefinitely, burning tokens and blocking the worker.
storing agent context in a Python dict means a worker crash loses all progress. Always persist to a durable store after each step.
if a worker dies mid-execution, the queue message is re-delivered after the visibility timeout. Without idempotency guards the agent starts from scratch, potentially duplicating side effects.
if a worker exception leaves the task in
status forever, the client will poll indefinitely. Always write
Persist state after every significant step
— not just at the end. Partial results are valuable and allow crash recovery.
timestamp every 10–30 seconds so a monitoring job can detect stuck workers.
Set a step budget and a wall-clock timeout
— protect against runaway agents and unexpected cost.
— if the step has already been recorded in the state store, skip it rather than re-executing.
Emit structured logs with the task_id
in every log line — this is the thread that lets you trace an execution across worker restarts.
and have the worker check a
flag at the start of each loop iteration.

Horizontal scale is simple: start more worker processes consuming the same queue. Each worker is stateless — it reads all context from the state store. Queue depth acts as a natural back-pressure signal: if depth is rising, add workers; if workers are idle, reduce them.

For token-heavy tasks (large context windows, many steps), costs scale with agent complexity rather than concurrency. Profile a representative task to estimate per-task token spend before setting pricing or usage limits. Use
for repeated system-prompt portions to reduce cost significantly when the same instructions appear in every LLM call.

State store latency matters at high queue throughput: every step writes a heartbeat plus state. Use an in-memory store (Redis) for heartbeats and a durable store (Postgres) for final results, or use Redis with AOF persistence for both.
the classic Python async task stack. Celery handles worker management, retries, and result backends out of the box.
durable execution frameworks that persist the full call stack across crashes without manual checkpointing.
managed queue and compute; visibility timeout provides at-least-once delivery.
Redis-backed job queue with priority, rate limiting, and repeatable jobs.
lightweight async worker library backed by Redis, suitable for smaller deployments.
Validate input at the API layer
before enqueuing — a malformed goal string should not reach the worker.
— agents that run code or write files need strict sandbox boundaries to prevent privilege escalation.
— never embed API keys in the task payload; inject them via environment variables at worker startup.
— log every tool invocation and its arguments to a tamper-evident store for post-hoc review.
after N retry attempts, move failed tasks to a dead-letter queue so they can be inspected and replayed manually.
— prevent a single client from flooding the queue and starving other users.
Long-running agents decouple task execution from the HTTP request lifecycle — the client gets a task ID immediately and retrieves results later via polling or webhook.
State must live in a durable external store, not in the worker process — this is what enables crash recovery and horizontal scaling.
Heartbeating is the mechanism by which a monitoring system detects stuck workers and re-queues their tasks.
Every step should be idempotent — side effects must be deduplicated because the same step may be executed more than once after a crash and re-queue.
A step budget and wall-clock timeout are mandatory — they protect against runaway cost and deadlocked workers.
Worker scale is independent of API scale — add or remove workers without touching the front-end, allowing throughput to follow demand.
For maximum reliability with minimal manual checkpointing, consider a durable execution framework (Temporal, Inngest) instead of a raw queue + worker.
